questionText: The number of projects handled by Company B in the year 2004 was 320 and in 2006 was 190. What is the percentage decrease in the number of projects handled by Company B from 2004 to 2006?

steps.explanation: Identify the values: projects in 2004 = 320, projects in 2006 = 190
Apply the percentage decrease formula

solution.shortcut: Decrease fraction = 130/320 = 13/32; multiply by 100 to get %
solution.commonMistake: Using the new value (190) as the denominator instead of the original (320)

modelName: Percentage Decrease from Line Graph

questionText: The number of projects handled by Company A in 2007 was 300. In 2008 it increased by 20% over 2007, and in 2009 it increased by 5% over 2008. What is the number of projects handled by Company A in 2009?

steps.explanation: Number of projects in 2008 = 120% of 300
Number of projects in 2009 = 105% of 360

solution.commonMistake: Adding percentages directly: 300 × 1.25 instead of applying sequentially

modelName: Successive Percentage Increase from Line Graph

questionText: The number of projects handled by Company A in 2003 and 2004 together was 180 + 230 = 410. The number of projects handled by Company B in 2005 and 2007 together was 210 + 350 = 560. What is the difference between these two totals?

steps.explanation: Total projects by Company A in 2003 and 2004
Total projects by Company B in 2005 and 2007
Required difference

solution.shortcut: Direct subtraction of the two sums
solution.commonMistake: Reading wrong years from the graph

modelName: Difference of Grouped Totals from Line Graph

questionText: From a line graph showing visas issued for Country A and Country B over different months: Visas issued for Country A and Country B in April were 310 and 350 respectively, and in June were 320 and 250 respectively. What is the difference between total visas issued in April and June combined for Countries A and B?

steps.explanation: Total visas for Country A and Country B in April
Total visas for Country A and Country B in June
Required difference

solution.shortcut: Sum each month's visas, then find difference
solution.commonMistake: Comparing individual countries instead of combined totals

modelName: Difference of Monthly Totals from Line Graph

questionText: From a line graph showing visas issued for Country B: Visas issued for Country B in March = 300, May = 280, July = 320, August = 360. What is the average number of visas issued for Country B in these four months?

steps.explanation: Sum of visas for Country B in the four months
Average = Total / Number of months

solution.commonMistake: Dividing by the wrong number of months

modelName: Average from Line Graph

questionText: From a line graph showing visas issued for Country A over months: Visas issued for Country A in March = 400 and this was 80% of the number in February. Visas issued for Country A in April = 360. What is the ratio of visas in February to visas in April?

steps.explanation: Find visas in February: March value (400) is 80% of February value
Required ratio = February : April

solution.shortcut: If March = 80% of February, then February = March × (100/80)
solution.commonMistake: Taking ratio as 400:360 without first finding February value

modelName: Reverse Percentage and Ratio from Line Graph

questionText: From a line graph showing visas issued for Country A over months: Visas in May = 360 and in July = 250. What is the approximate percentage decrease in visas from May to July?

steps.explanation: Identify the values: May = 360, July = 250
Calculate percentage decrease

solution.commonMistake: Dividing by July value (250) instead of May value (360)

modelName: Percentage Decrease from Line Graph

questionText: From a line graph showing visas: Visas issued for Country B in March = 300 and visas issued for Country A in June = 320. What percentage less are the Country B March visas compared to Country A June visas?

steps.explanation: Country B March = 300, Country A June = 320
Required percentage = (difference / Country A June) × 100

solution.commonMistake: Using Country B value (300) as the denominator

modelName: Percentage Comparison from Line Graph

questionText: The exports (in crore Rs.) of Companies X, Y, Z in various years are given. In 2003: X=30, Y=80, Z=60. In 2008: X=50, Y=100, Z=80. What was the difference between the average exports of the three companies in 2003 and the average exports in 2008?

steps.explanation: Average exports of X, Y, Z in 2003
Average exports of X, Y, Z in 2008
Difference between averages

solution.shortcut: Difference of averages = (Sum in 2008 - Sum in 2003)/3 = (230-170)/3 = 60/3 = 20
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to divide by 3 to get the average

modelName: Difference of Averages from Line Graph

questionText: The exports (in crore Rs.) of Companies X and Y in various years are: 2003: X=30, Y=80; 2004: X=60, Y=40; 2005: X=40, Y=60; 2006: X=70, Y=60; 2007: X=100, Y=80; 2008: X=50, Y=100; 2009: X=120, Y=140. In which year was the difference between the exports from Companies X and Y the minimum?

steps.explanation: Calculate the absolute difference between X and Y exports for each year
Identify the minimum difference

solution.shortcut: Check differences year by year; 2006 gives |70-60|=10 which is least
solution.commonMistake: Confusing X and Y values or reading the wrong year from the graph

modelName: Minimum Difference Year from Line Graph

questionText: The exports (in crore Rs.) of Companies X, Y, Z in various years: 2003: X=30, Y=80, Z=60 (Total=170); 2004: X=60, Y=40, Z=90 (Total=190); 2005: X=40, Y=60, Z=120 (Total=220). For which of the following pairs of years are the total exports from the three companies together equal?

steps.explanation: Calculate total exports for 2003, 2004, 2005
Also compute 2006 total: X=70, Y=60, Z=90
Compare totals to find equal pair
steps.result: 2005 and 2006 are the pair with equal totals

solution.shortcut: Scan totals for matching values; 2005 and 2006 both give 220
solution.commonMistake: Stopping at the first few years without checking 2006

modelName: Equal Totals Pair from Line Graph

questionText: From a line graph showing the number of calories burnt by Person A and Person B on different days of the week: On Wednesday, A burnt 90 calories and B burnt 125 calories; on Tuesday, A burnt 185 calories and B burnt 100 calories. What is the ratio of total calories burnt by A and B together on Wednesday to that on Tuesday?

steps.explanation: Total calories burnt by A and B together on Wednesday
Total calories burnt by A and B together on Tuesday
Required ratio = Wednesday : Tuesday

solution.shortcut: GCD of 215 and 285 is 5; divide both by 5 to get 43:57
solution.commonMistake: Not simplifying the ratio to its lowest terms

modelName: Ratio of Daily Totals from Line Graph

questionText: From a line graph showing calories burnt by Person A and Person B on various days: On Saturday, A burnt 180 calories and B burnt 145 calories. If on the next Saturday A increases her calories by 10% and B increases by 20%, what is the total number of calories burnt by both on that next Saturday?

steps.explanation: Calories by A on next Saturday = 180 × 110/100
Calories by B on next Saturday = 145 × 120/100
Total calories burnt on that Saturday

solution.shortcut: Multiply original values by respective growth factors, then add
solution.commonMistake: Adding the percentages and applying to the sum rather than applying individually

modelName: Total After Percentage Increase from Line Graph

questionText: From a line graph showing the number of calories burnt by Person A on different days: A burnt 185 calories on Thursday, 90 calories on Wednesday, and 160 calories on Friday. What is the total number of calories burnt by A on these three days?

steps.explanation: Sum calories burnt by A on Thursday, Wednesday, and Friday

solution.commonMistake: Reading the wrong values from the graph for different days

modelName: Sum of Values from Line Graph

questionText: From a line graph showing calories burnt by Person B on various days: B burnt 100 calories on Tuesday, 125 calories on Wednesday, and 55 calories on Thursday (total for Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday = 280). If the average calories burnt by B over three days including Saturday is 125, how many calories did B burn on Saturday?

steps.explanation: Given that average calories = 125 over 3 days, total calories = 3 × 125
Calories on the other two days combined = 280 (Tuesday+Wednesday+Thursday or a sub-total given)

solution.shortcut: Saturday calories = Total - Sum of other days = 375 - 280 = 95
solution.commonMistake: Confusing total with average; using 125 directly instead of computing 3 × 125 first

modelName: Finding Missing Value Using Average from Line Graph

questionText: From a line graph showing calories burnt by Person A and Person B: On a particular day, A burnt 75 calories and B burnt 60 calories. What percentage is the calories burnt by B of the calories burnt by A?

steps.explanation: Required percentage = (B's calories / A's calories) × 100

solution.commonMistake: Dividing A by B instead of B by A

modelName: Percentage Comparison from Line Graph

questionText: From a line graph showing the number of watches sold in Town X and Town Y over months: Watches sold in Town X in January = 120, February = 140, March = 180, June = 240. What is the average number of watches sold in Town X in these four months?

steps.explanation: Total watches sold in Town X in January, February, March and June
Average = Total / Number of months

solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to divide by 4 or including wrong months

modelName: Average from Line Graph

questionText: From a line graph showing watches sold: In April, Town Y sold 210 watches and Town X sold 150 watches. By what percentage are the watches sold in Town Y more than those sold in Town X in April?

steps.explanation: Difference = Town Y - Town X
Percentage more = (Difference / Town X) × 100

solution.commonMistake: Using Town Y (210) as the denominator instead of Town X (150)

modelName: Percentage More/Less from Line Graph

questionText: From a line graph showing watches sold in Town Y over months: In February, Town Y sold 120 watches and in May, Town Y sold 230 watches. What is the percentage increase in watches sold in Town Y from February to May?

steps.explanation: Increase = May - February
Percentage increase = (Increase / February) × 100

solution.commonMistake: Dividing by May value (230) instead of the original February value (120)

modelName: Percentage Increase from Line Graph

questionText: From a line graph showing watches sold in Town X: May = 180, July = 110% of May, January = 120. What is the ratio of watches sold in July to that in January?

steps.explanation: Watches sold in Town X in May = 180; July = 110% of May
Watches sold in Town X in January = 120
Required ratio = July : January

solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to compute July from the given percentage of May

modelName: Ratio After Percentage Increase from Line Graph

questionText: From a line graph showing watches sold in Town X and Town Y: In June, Town X sold 240 and Town Y sold 180 watches. In March, Town X sold 180 and Town Y sold 150 watches. What is the difference between the total watches sold in both towns in June and in March?

steps.explanation: Total watches sold in both towns in June
Total watches sold in both towns in March
Required difference

solution.shortcut: Sum each month's total then subtract
solution.commonMistake: Comparing individual towns across months instead of combined totals

modelName: Difference of Monthly Totals from Line Graph

questionText: From a line graph showing profit percentages of Companies A and B over years: Company B had profit% = 35% in 2009 with expenditure Rs. 12 lakhs, and profit% = 50% in 2010 with expenditure Rs. 14.5 lakhs. What is the total income of Company B in 2009 and 2010 together? [Profit% = (Income - Expenditure) / Expenditure × 100]

steps.explanation: Find income of Company B in 2009 using profit% formula: 35 = (I - 12)/12 × 100
Find income of Company B in 2010 using profit% formula: 50 = (I - 14.5)/14.5 × 100
Total income in 2009 and 2010

solution.shortcut: Income = Expenditure × (100 + Profit%) / 100; sum both years
solution.commonMistake: Confusing profit% formula; using (Income/Expenditure × 100) instead of ((Income-Expenditure)/Expenditure × 100)

modelName: Total Income from Profit Percentage (Line Graph)

questionText: From a line graph showing profit percentages of Companies A and B: In 2012, Company A had profit% = 30% with expenditure Rs. 3 lakhs, and Company B had profit% = 40% with expenditure Rs. 4 lakhs. What is the ratio of income of Company A to income of Company B in 2012?

steps.explanation: Income of Company A in 2012: P% = (I-E)/E × 100 → 30 = (I-3)/3 × 100
Income of Company B in 2012: 40 = (I-4)/4 × 100
Required ratio = Income of A : Income of B

solution.commonMistake: Using profit instead of income for the ratio

modelName: Ratio of Incomes from Profit Percentage (Line Graph)

questionText: From a line graph showing profit percentages of Companies A and B over years: If information about profit percentage of Company A in all the years is not completely given, can the required value be determined?

steps.explanation: The solution cannot be determined because profit percentage of Company A in all the years are not given in the graph
steps.calculation: Insufficient data — profit% of Company A for all required years not available

solution.shortcut: Check if all required data is available before attempting calculations
solution.commonMistake: Attempting to answer with partial data leading to incorrect results

modelName: Data Sufficiency from Line Graph
